text: The laparoscopic view shows the careful dissection of the pelvic sidewall structures, focusing on the anatomical landmarks such as the ureter forming the medial border of the parorectal space. The surgeon resects the obturator lymph nodes while maintaining awareness of the external iliac vessels as the lateral border and the levator ani muscle as the base of the space.

The laparoscopic view shows the dissection in the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane with the rectum still in situ. A large metastatic obturator lymph node is visible within the left pelvic sidewall. The surgeon opens medially from the ureter to access the lymph node, but due to its size and location, the parorectal space is opened, exposing surrounding anatomical structures. Dissection proceeds lateral to the ureter for better visualization.

The video provides an overview of the parorectal structures, highlighting the section parallel to the external iliac artery that safely opens the parorectal space. The external iliac vessels are visible, with a branch from the external iliac vein noted, showing anatomical variance. The vein appears to be the confluence of the superior physical vein and the obturator vein, typically ending in the internal iliac vein. The pelvic parietal fascia, named after the muscle it covers, forms the lateral border of the pelvic sidewall, with a fascial thickening from the obturator internus fascia to which the levator ani muscle attaches, forming the base of the parorectal space.

The surgeon carefully dissects the pelvic sidewall to mobilize the enlarged lymph node, which is closely related to the obturator nerve. The lymph node is carefully separated from the nerve, providing a clear view of the parorectal space and its structures. Once fully mobilized, the lymph node is placed in an extraction bag for removal with the rectal specimen. The rectal resection is then completed laparoscopically.
